table_of_content.title: Introduction
text: Introduction

The 2000 paper «Value Investing: The Use of Historical Financial Statement Information to Separate Winners from Losers» by Joseph D. Piotroski gave value investors a useful tool in the face of the F-score, the signal equal to the sum of nine binary signals based on the company’s financial performance. Since then, the F-score became one of the classic metrics for taking investment decisions concerning high book-to-market (BM) stocks.

Having said that, the F-score is not indeed a panacea and does leave room for skepticism. The concern is related to the fact that Piotroski assessed his model on the timeline from 1976 to 1996 when the market was in the bull-run phase: S&P 500 return equaled to 634.44% during these 20 years. Although Piotroski used not only simple returns, but also market-adjusted returns (MARs) in his work, capturing value in the bull market should have been dramatically different from doing so in the bear market.

This critical stance was taken in the 2011 research by Melissa Woodley, Steven T. Jones, and James P. Reburn who assessed the efficiency of the F-score from 1997 to 2008, when the market lived through the dot.com bubble blow, recession of 2001, and the Global financial crisis, and the resulting return of the S&P 500 only showed modest 14.89%. The next research published by this group explained the discrepancy by the influence of the fear index VIX on the strategy: the differences between the returns of the high F-score firms and the low F-score firms and the high F-score firms and high BM companies were statistically significant only when the mean VIX during 30 days prior to portfolio formation did not exceed 20.

What I can add to this discussion is questioning how efficient the model is when the broad market is growing, the VIX is low enough, but the macroeconomic conditions do not at all favor the well-being of the high BM firms – the firms, the valuation of which is the utmost goal of the F-score. In 2022, Indian monetary authorities began their campaign against inflation by substantially raising the interest rates. However, the robust internal demand and the opening of the economy after lifting the COVID-19 restrictions allowed the market to end the year higher with a 4.4% growth. The VIX in 2022 was also rather low, supposing that the model should work. Having said that, high BM firms are usually financially distressed, and, I believe, the monetary tightening is supposed to take its toll on them, therefore, distorting the efficiency of the model.

The goal of my paper is to assess the efficiency of the F-score amidst this macroeconomic turbulence in India, 2022, and develop subsequent recommendation for value investors based on my findings.  In order to achieve this goal, I have to complete numerous objectives allocated in three thematic chapters. The first chapter «F-score and its Efficiency» explains the Piotroski’s formula, provides traits of the high BM firms, proves the long-term efficiency of the F-score in the emerging markets, and, finally, criticizes the signal for its dependence on the VIX. The second chapter «Macroeconomic Turbulence in India 2022» describes the economic problems in the chosen country during the period of research and explains their influence on the high BM firms and the Indian stock market. The third and the last chapter of the main body «Empirical Research» provides the detailed methodology of the research, lists the potential limitations, and examines the efficiency of the F-score with the help of both parametric and non-parametric tests. Conclusion section provides the implication for value investors based on the results obtained in the research.

The subject of the research is 1 069 high BM companies, and the objects are their subsequent annual returns in 2022 and their subsequent F-scores on January 1st, 2022. The brief methodology of the empirical research is as follows. First, I assess the impact of the F-score at the beginning of the year on the stock returns by means of linear regression. Then, I create two portfolios of the high F-score firms (potential winners) and the low F-score firms (potential losers) in order to evaluate the efficiency of the investment strategy based on the F-score. High F-score and low F-score portfolios are compared with the use of t-test and bootstrapping; high F-score and broad portfolios are compared with the use of t-test, and low F-score and broad portfolios are compared with the use of t-test and bootstrapping. The high F-score sample includes 157 companies, and the low F-score sample includes only 17 companies; therefore, bootstrapping is applied to the low F-score portfolio as an additional safeguard against modest sample size. Finally, I run regression analysis to evaluate whether average trading volume during the year or firm’s market capitalization at the beginning of the year could shift the distribution of returns of the high F-score companies.

sub_elements.title: 1.1. Joseph D. Piotroski’s F-score
text: In his well-known paper «Value Investing: The Use of Historical Financial Statement Information to Separate Winners from Losers», Joseph D. Piotroski [2000] examined whether a simple accounting-based fundamental strategy, when applied to a broad portfolio of high BM firms, can shift the distribution of returns earned by an investor.

In his work, Piotroski divided US companies in five quintiles depending on their BM ratios in the years prior to portfolio formation. He concentrated on the fifth quintile, i.e., firms with the highest BM ratios. Overall, his sample consisted of 14 043 companies on the timeline of 20 years: from 1976 to 1996. According to the researcher, less than 44% of high BM companies earn positive MARs in the two years after the portfolio formation. Naturally, the question of why these mostly distressed companies laid in the foundation of the study arises.

In order to explain his choice, Piotroski addressed the prior researches about high BM. According to Fama and French [1992, p. 428], BM is characterized as a variable capturing financial distress, and thus the subsequent returns represent a fair compensation for risk. Low ROE is associated with high BM [Fama and French, 1992, p. 441; Penman, 1991, p. 250]. According to Lakonishok, Shleifer, and Vishny [1994, p. 1575], high BM is a signal of the market ignorance and excessive pessimism. Further positive earnings surprises may unravel this pessimism [LaPorta et al., 1997, p. 1740]. If high BM firms are compared with the so-called growth stocks, most of the predictability in returns of the latter ones is driven by momentum strength or weakness [Asness, 1997], whereas high BM stocks are inherently more conducive to financial statements analysis. In the end, Piotroski generalized all of the above facts about high BM companies in three points that justify his area of the research:
High BM firms are often neglected (ignorance is expressed in the lack of analysts’ forecasts and stock recommendations);
High BM firms have limited access to most “informal” information dissemination channels, and their voluntary disclosures may not be viewed as credible given their poor recent performance;
Therefore, financial statements are the most reliable and the most accessible source of information.

Finally, Piotroski wrote that his strategy is based on the principal formulated by Frankel and Lee [1998, p. 315]: investors should purchase stocks, whose prices appear to be lagging fundamental values. The researcher intended to capture this lag by means of pure analysis of financial statements of the studied companies. Piotroski lists the following strategies based on the discussed market’s inability to value the company correctly with the earnings reports: post-earnings announcement drift [Bernard and Thomas, 1989-1990, p. 32], accruals [Sloan, 1996, p. 314], seasoned equity offerings [Loughran and Ritter, 1995, p. 46], share repurchases [Ikenberry, Lakonishok, and Vermaelen, 1995, p. 206], dividend omissions/decreases [Michaely, Thaler, and Womack, 1995, p. 605]. For the explanation for the distress of the average high BM firm, Piotroski cites Fama and French [1992, p. 429]: it may be due to declining or persistently low margins, profits, cash flows, and liquidity, or due to rising or high levels of financial leverage.

Taking these parameters in consideration, Piotroski designed a formula (F-score) equal to the sum of nine binary values representing three areas of financial condition: profitability, financial leverage/liquidity, and operating efficiency:
F_SCORE=s=19signal ,
where signal is the binary value equal either to zero if it is “bad”, or to one if it is “good”.
The first area, profitability, consists of four signals: F_ROA, F_CFO, F_ΔROA, F_ACCRUAL.
ROA=Net IncomeAssets at the Beginning of the Year
CFO=Cash Flow from OperationsAssets at the Beginning of the year
If ROA and CFO are positive, F_ROA and F_CFO equal to one, otherwise they equal zero.
∆ROA=ROAcurrent year-ROAprior year
If ΔROA is positive, F_ΔROA equals one, otherwise it equals zero.
Accrual=Net Income-Cash Flow from Operations Assets at the Beginning of the Year=ROA-CFO
If Accrual is negative, F_ACCRUAL equals one, otherwise it equal zero.
The second area dedicated to the financing of the business includes three signals: F_ΔLEVER, F_ΔLIQUID, and F_EQ_OFFER.
∆Lever=LTDcurrent yearTAcurrent year-LTDprior yearTAprior year
If ΔLever is negative, F_ΔLEVER equals one, otherwise it equals zero.
∆Liquid=∆Current Ratio=CAcurrent yearCLcurrent year-CAprior yearCLprior year
If ΔLiquid is positive, F_ ΔLIQUID equals one, otherwise it equals zero.
F_EQ_OFFER equals one if the firm did not issue common equity in the year preceding portfolio formation, otherwise it equals zero.
The last area describing operating efficiency provides only two signals: F_ΔMARGIN and F_ΔTURN.
∆Margin=Gross Margincurrent yearTotal Assetscurrent year-Gross Marginprior yearTotal Assetsprior year
If ΔMargin is positive, F_ΔMARGIN equals one, otherwise it equals zero.
∆Turn=Total Salescurrent periodTotal Assetscurrent period-Total Salesprior yearTotal Assetsprior year
If ΔTurn is positive, F_ΔTURN equals one, otherwise it equals zero.
To reiterate, the final equation is:
F_SCORE=F_ROA+F_CFO+F_∆ROA+F_ACCRUAL+F_∆LEVER+F_∆LIQUID+F_EQ_OFFER+F_∆MARGIN+F_∆TURN
F-score may take values from zero to nine; the higher is the value for the high BM company, the stronger is the return the stock is supposed to produce.

After describing the formula, Piotroski matched the stocks with their subsequent one-year (two-year) returns. The returns were recorded from the beginning of the fifth month after the end of the company’s fiscal year. The fifth month was chosen in order to ensure that all necessary financial information was available. If the company delisted, then the return was regarded to be zero.

In order to check the efficiency of the F-score, Piotroski conducted numerous statistical tests. Spearmen correlation between the F-score and the stock return equaled 0.124, but Piotroski was also interested in estimating the stock return difference between the best and the worst companies, according to their F-score values. The researcher classified the companies with the respective F-scores of zero or one as the low F-score firms, and the firms with the respective F-score of eight or nine as high F-score firms. Overall, there were 396 low F-score firms and 1 448 high F-score firms in the population.

Piotroski compared the returns of the high F-score companies against those of the low F-score companies using classic parametric t-test and non-parametric bootstrapping approach. t-statistics for the one-year raw returns equaled 5.594, indicating the significant difference (the mean return of the low F-score firms equaled 7.8%, while the mean return of the high F-score firms equaled 31.3%). Bootstrap results also confirmed the difference within the means, medians, 10th, 25th, 75, and 90th percentiles. Then, Piotroski used the same statistical procedures to compare the returns of the high F-score companies against the return of the broad portfolio of high BM stocks. t-statistics for the one-year raw returns equaled 3.279, once again pointing at the significant difference (the mean return of the high F-score firms equaled 31.3%, while the mean return of the broad portfolio of the high BM firms equaled 23.9%). Bootstrap statistics corroborated the results of the t-test within the means, medians, 10th, 25th, and 75th percentiles.

Having proved that the F-score is capable of discriminating poor-performing stocks from those well-performing, Piotroski went further to evaluate whether other variables, such as firm’s size, share turnover, and analyst coverage, are capable of influencing the returns.

In order to assess the impact of firm’s size on the returns, Piotroski split 75 000 firms available on Compustat in three equal groups, depending on the market capitalizations. Then he applied the high BM condition, highlighting 14 043 companies. As a result, 8 302 firms (59.12%) were in the bottom-third of market capitalization, while 3 906 (27.81%) and 1 835 (13.07%) belonged to the medium- and high-cap-thirds, respectively. The application of the F-score proved to be the most effective within the small-cap portfolio, where the returns’ difference between the high F-score and low F-score firms equaled to 27% at the 1% level of significance. Within the medium-cap firms, this difference equaled to approximately 17.3% at the same level of significance. Finally, the capability of the strategy to differentiate winners from losers within the large-cap stocks was either insignificant or only marginally significant at the 5% or 10% level.

Analogical procedure was used to assess the impact of the trading volume on the strategy. Out of 14 043 studied firms, 7661 belonged to the low volume portfolio, 3664 – to the medium volume one, and 2718 – to the high volume one. In all three cases, the returns’ difference between the high and low F-score companies was statistically significant at the 1% level. The difference was the highest in the low volume portfolio (23.9%). In the medium and high-volume portfolios, the differences equaled to 17.5% and 20.3%, respectively.

As for the analyst following, Piotroski had two hypothesis that he managed to prove with his research. First, high BM firms should display low levels of analyst coverage, and, second, the ability to earn strong returns should be negatively related to the amount of analyst coverage provided. Out of 14 043 high BM companies, only 5 317 (37.8%) had the analyst coverage in the year preceding portfolio formation. Those firms without analyst coverage managed to outperform the value-weighted market index by 10.1%, whereas their counterparts followed by the analysts simply produced the market return. When F-score was applied to discriminate winners from losers in these two groups, the differences between high and low F-score companies were significant within both portfolios at the 1% level; however, the portfolio without analyst coverage demonstrated a stronger reaction of 27.7% compared to the 11.4% of the portfolio with analyst following.

In the end of the paper, Piotroski performed sensitivity analysis via applying the F-score to the portfolios distributed upon financial health and historical change in profitability conditions. Financial health was evaluated using Altman’s z-score, and change in profitability was evaluated using change in ROA. The studied high BM firms were assigned to three partitions depending on financial health and to three partitions depending on change in profitability similarly to how they were distributed depending on firm’s size. The values of Altman’s z-score and ΔROA were based on the preceding fiscal year’s cutoffs. Consistent with the theory about high BM firms, 7 919 (56.4%) firms from the studied sample ended up in the high distress partition. 4 432 (30.8%) firms were in the medium distress, and only 1 792 (12.8%) were in the low distress section. In all cases, F-score criteria was effective at separating winners from losers at the 1% level of significance. The subsequent returns difference between the high F-score firms and low F-score firms was as follows: 18.7% for high distress, 29.4% for medium distress, and 36.3% for low distress. As for the portfolio formation based on ΔROA, 3 265 (23.3%) companies were in the high ΔROA partition, 4 391 (31.3%) – in the medium ΔROA, and 6 387 (45.5%) – in the low ΔROA partition. The strategy passed this sensitivity test too: in all three partitions the differences between the high F-score firms and the low F-score firms were statistically significant at the 1% level. They equaled to 30.8%, 13%, and 21.1% for the high, medium, and low ΔROA partitions, respectively.

sub_elements.title: 1.2. Efficiency of the F-score in the Emerging Markets
text: Since Piotroski’s F-score is based on the fundamental parameters influencing stock returns, it is intuitively clear that the trading strategy based on the F-score will be efficient in the emerging markets in the long-run just like in the US market at a distance (Piotroski examined US stock returns from 1976 to 1996). However, in order to add rigor to this work, I describe the research by Charles Hyde [2014] who evaluated the F-score across all countries, including India, in the MSCI emerging markets index.

Unlike Piotroski, Hyde did not restrict his attention to deep value stocks in his analysis, since emerging markets are likely to be slower to price in new information for all stocks. Hyde cites Mohr [2012] who found out that the F-score effectively discriminates between high and low return stocks among growth stocks as well. Therefore, Hyde hypothesized that for all stocks, irrespective of whether they belong to growth or value category, the lower information efficiency of emerging markets could plausibly result in the F-score having even more power in emerging markets than in developed ones.

Overall, Hyde’s sample included 99 658 stock-date observations from January 2000 to July 2011 for six-month holding strategy and to January 2011 for 12-month holding strategy. The subsequent returns were calculated from the beginning of the first month to the end of the last month in the holding period. In order to calculate the MAR, Hyde subtracted the respective index return from the stock return for the same period. 75% of the sample were occupied by firms with the F-score value of four to seven. The year-to-year variations in this proportion were insignificant. High F-score firms with the respective F-score value of at least eight equaled 9.5% of the sample, and low F-score firms with the respective F-score value of no more than two equaled to 8% of the sample (Hyde increased the upper boundary of the low F-score range in order to increase the quantity of the studied low F-score firms). Interesting, the lowest mean F-score was registered in the years 2000, 2009, and 2010, when the global economic growth was the weakest in the period of research.

In order to check whether the selected stock returns are conditioned on firm’s value, Hyde divided the sample in three groups depending on the BM ratio: bottom 20% (expensive), medium 60%, and top 20% (undervalued). Afterwards, the high F-score companies were assigned to these partitions depending on their BM values. The sixth-month (twelve-month) MARs were -1.07% (-1.53%), 0.83% (0.84%), and 3.51% (2.06%) for the bottom, medium, and top partitions, respectively. This finding of Hyde stresses the importance of coherent consideration of both the BM value and the F-score and refutes the previous research of Mohr [2012] claiming that the F-score may also be efficient for discriminating winners from losers in the growth stocks. Therefore, in my research, I do take BM value into account.

Returning to the efficiency of the F-score in the emerging markets, the sixth-month (twelve-month) MARs were 3.33% (3.41%) and -0.77% (-0.95%) for the high F-score firms and the low F-score firms, respectively. The sixth-month (twelve-month) premium of the high F-score MAR minus low F-score MAR equaled 4.1% (4.36%). This difference was significant at the 1% level. Except for the mean premium, Hyde calculated the value for separate countries and groups of countries that he studied. In India the return difference between the high and low F-score companies equaled 13.09%, the value which is the second largest after the one of SE Asia group of countries (14.2%). The only country where this premium was negative was Brazil with the difference being equal to -13.49%.

It is noteworthy that Hyde also calculated the mean premium for separate years from 2000 to 2011. The return difference between the high and low F-score companies was positive in all years, except for 2000, 2005, and 2009 when the premium equaled -4.8%, -5.3%, and -13.2%, respectively. As is known, 2000 and 2009 were years of financials shock and economic turbulence; therefore, the economic environment and the destructive market losses may possibly distort the efficiency of F-score.

To reiterate, the F-score can successfully be applied to most emerging countries (except for Brazil), and to the Indian market in particular, where it demonstrated extraordinary efficiency in separating winners from losers. Hyde’s research underpins the necessity to combine the F-score with the BM ratio, stressing that the formula invented by Piotroski serves the deep-value stocks. Finally, Hyde showed that the strategy may fail in the years of financial harshness.

sub_elements.title: 1.3. The “Fear Index” and the Effectiveness of the Piotroski Model
text: The 2011 paper by Melissa Woodley, Steven T. Jones, and James P. Reburn questioned the efficiency of the F-score in separating winners from losers. The researchers examined the sample in the period from 1997 to 2008, straight after Piotroski’s studied period ending in 1996. This time interval contained several major economic shocks: the infamous dot.com bubble, recession of 2001, and Global financial crisis of 2008. The results of the model were poor, as high F-score companies sufficiently underperformed compared to the broad portfolio of value stocks. The researchers explained this phenomenon by stating that when more investors had learned about the model, its predicting power decreased.

Eight years later, in 2019, researchers [Woodley, Jones, and Reburn, 2019] returned with another article offering a more accurate explanation for what might have happened to the formula. The new research expanded the studied period to the one from 1989 to 2013, and included 19 200 observations of high BM firms (firms in the highest BM quintile) and their subsequent annual returns recorded after five months from the portfolio formation. The difference between MARs of the high F-score firms and all value companies equaled to insignificant -2.48%, and the difference between MARs of the high F-score firms and the low F-score firms equaled 13.06% that was significant at the 5% level. In order to explain the inefficiency of the formula in the high versus all case, it is necessary to take a closer look at the related percentiles. The differences equaled to 15.9%, 11.46%, 5.92%, -3.52%, and -22.74% in the 10th, 25th, 50th, 75th, and 90th percentiles, respectively. Therefore, an average high F-score firm is relatively less likely to produce either extremely poor, or extremely good results.

Woodley, Jones, and Reburn hypothesized that the F-score’s ability to discriminate winners from losers is dependent on the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX). The VIX, introduced by Whaley [1993, p. 72], measures expected near-term volatility implied by stock index option prices. This index is often used as a measure of investor fear [Copeland and Copeland, 1999, p. 73; Whaley, 2009, p. 99]. In line with Merton’s [1980, p. 332] assumption that with the increase in the expected returns’ volatility, investors seek the higher risk premia, Durand, Lim, and Zumwald [2011, p. 424] proved that higher VIX levels are associated with increases in both the market risk premium and the value premium. Copeland and Copeland [1999, p. 79] found that when DVIX is high (low), value stocks outperform (underperform) growth stocks. Boscaljon, Filbeck, and Zhao [2011, p. 41] corroborate this thesis, provided that the holding period exceeds 30 days.

Woodley, Jones, and Reburn divided their sample in three partitions, depending on the VIX levels in 30 days prior to portfolio formation. If an average VIX during the period was below 20, then a firm joined the low-VIX partition. If an average VIX during the period was between 20 and 30, then a firm was added to the medium-VIX partition. Finally, if an average VIX during the period was above 30, a firm belonged to the high-VIX partition. Having assigned the firms to these regimes, the researchers obtained and compared the returns for all value stocks and high and low F-score companies.

In the low-VIX section, mean MAR for all value firms, high F-score firms, and low F-score firms equaled 5.35%, 11.37%, and -11.37%, respectively. The 6.03% and 22.7% differences between high F-score and all value companies and high and low F-score companies, respectively, were statistically significant at the 1% level. In the medium-VIX section, mean MAR for all value firms, high F-score firms, and low F-score firms equaled 20.48%, 12.57%, and -0.62%, respectively. In this case, the -7.91% and 13.19% differences between high F-score and all value companies and high and low F-score companies, respectively, were statistically insignificant. Finally, in the high-VIX section, mean MAR for all value firms, high F-score firms, and low F-score firms equaled 84.4%, 29.79%, and 70.3%, respectively. The -54.61% difference between high F-score and all value companies was statistically significant at the 1% level; however, obviously, not in favor of the high F-score firms and, consequently, the model. The -40.51% difference between high F-score and low F-score firms was statistically insignificant.

To conclude, the results obtained by the researchers point at the Piotroski’s model’s inability to work successfully in times, when the VIX levels are high. Hence, I provide VIX levels for India during 2022, since these values influence the research hypothesis (I have only one sample period, 2022; therefore, I cannot and do not compare the results for low-, medium-, and high-VIX periods. What is crucial is that during the portfolio formation VIX was low enough to suppose the F-score efficiency, according to the research cited here.)

table_of_content.title: CHAPTER 2. MACROECONOMIC TURBULENCE IN INDIA 2022
text: As is said above, the aim of this paper is to estimate the efficiency of F-score amidst macroeconomic turbulence; therefore, this chapter is meant to demonstrate how India in 2022 answers this purpose, i.e., prove the harshness of macroeconomic conditions throughout the year and why this harshness is supposed to take its toll on the selected stocks. In order to do this, I will briefly describe how India is connected with the global economy in order to demonstrate why global financial problems in 2022 were critical, assess the dynamics of quantitative fundamental parameters of the economy (e.g., quarterly GDP, interest rates, monetary mass M2 etc.) and will, ultimately, show their influence on the Indian stock market in the technical picture review.

sub_elements.title: 2.1. India as a Part of Global Financial System
text: After gaining its independence from Britain in 1947, India had long remained an underdeveloped and overregulated economy with the corresponding poor financial sector which relied on a bank-based system for financial intermediation under the aegis of the public sector [Dhal, 2009, p. 784]. Country’s financial market was unappealing due to the shortage of liquidity and the absence of various financial derivatives available now. Obviously, this decrepit system could not successfully address the needs of the Indian households for saving and the needs of businesses for raising capital.

The first humble steps towards liberalization took place from 1985 to 1990 and comprised deregulation of the industrial and trade policies. A year later, in 1991, the financial crisis struck as a response to the structural shocks encountered by the system. In July 1991, International Monetary Fund stepped in with the program, and, later in the year, the World Bank intervened with the Structural Adjustment Loan. Therefore, the two international organizations set off progressive economic reforms in the country.

The reform in the financial sector occurred in 1991. The package encouraged Foreign Direct Investment (FDI), allowing up to 100% of FDI in particular industries. Furthermore, Indian companies were permitted to raise capital through American/global depositary receipts, foreign currency convertible bonds, and external commercial bonds. Security and Exchange Board of India was established to monitor the Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE). In 1993, the exchange rate of the national currency finally became flexible. Apparently, the newly adopted policies were quite successful in terms of capital raising. For instance, according to the World Bank, FDI net inflow in India increased from approximately USD 236.7 million in 1990 to USD 64.36 billion in 2020, when the inflow reached a peak (27 090% growth). Capitalization of listed domestic companies soared as well, increasing from USD 225.65 billion in 2000 to USD 2.6 trillion in 2020 (1 052% growth).

The major problems after the advent of liberalization emanated from the Global financial crisis of 2008. At that time, from 2007 to 2009, Indian stock market losses amounted to USD 9.9 trillion, the figure which is equal to nearly 50% of its total capitalization. Dhal [2009] studied the impact of this crisis on Indian involvement in globalization and, in particular, on the national stock market cointegration with the global markets (US, UK, and Japan) and the regional markets (Hong Kong and Singapore). He identified three theoretical reasons of why India would not turn away from globalization pass, however thorny it was seen in 2009. First, Indian economy needs access to the global liquidity to finance its growth. Second, political consensus about liberalization in the 90s was tedious and preceding negotiations were too protracted to make an easy step back. Finally, the collapse of 2008 happened due to inappropriate risk-pricing, rather than due to globalization issues [Kunt and Serven, 2009]. In his empirical research, Dhal analyzed two timeseries: from April 1993 to July 2009 (with financial crisis) and from April 1993 to January 18th 2008 (without financial crisis). He discovered the strong cointegration of the Indian market with Hong Kong, US, and UK and arrived to a conclusion that there was not any significant change in the markets’ short run adjustments to the long run pass due to the Global financial crisis. That said, the discovered correlation only attributed to the assets priced in US dollars, not national currencies, the phenomenon indicating the dominance of foreign capital in the Indian stock market.
Fig. 3 S&P 500 (orange) and BSE (blue), 1980-2022, time frame: 1-month, logarithmic scale
Source: TradingView: S&P BSE SENSEX // TradingView, 2023. – URL: https://ru.tradingview.com/chart/HLdPk7dH/?symbol=SP%3ASPX (extracted on 07.02.2023).

The further proof of India’s belonging to the global economy comes from Paksha [2009], whose research scrutinized the possible synchronization of India’s and US’s business cycles after the liberalization processes in the economy of the former country. In order to exclude the agricultural component that occupied a voluminous sector of the Indian economy in the past century, Paksha used indexes of industrial production for the two countries to estimate synchronization. The research showed that with the intensification of the economic liberalization, the countries’ business cycles became more and more synchronized, i.e., demonstrated similar macroeconomics tendencies simultaneously.

To reiterate, Indian economy as a whole and Indian financial market in particular are well cointegrated with the Western world. Shares traded on the Bombay Stock Exchange (nominated in dollars) usually perform similarly to their US counterparts, and foreign investors play a prominent role in the national market. India’s economy undergoes the macroeconomic trends of the globalized world, generally facing most economic ups and downs of the US, since the business cycles of the two countries are synchronized. The next section provides analysis of the Indian economy in 2022 showing that the country bit the bullet of the majority of the negative macrotrends of the year, including high inflation, followed by the hawkish actions of the Central bank, shrinking balance, decline in consumption, slowing down of the GDP, and the first signs of forthcoming recession, forecasting that the worst is yet to come.

sub_elements.title: 2.2. Indian Economy in 2022
text: In its report «Navigating the Storm» dating back to December 6th 2022, the World Bank claimed that “India’s economy demonstrated resilience despite a challenging external environment”, thus acknowledging the macroeconomic pressure, yet ranking the country better than most emerging markets. Among the challenges that the economy faced, the World Bank names tightening global monetary policy, slowing global growth, and elevated commodity prices. Strong performance of the national economy between July and September eventually led to the review of the GDP growth forecasts in 2022-2023 to the upside from 6.5% in October to 6.9% in December. The organization attributes India’s success to the robust domestic demand. I would add to this explanation that India was buying 33 times more Russian oil than it did in 2021, and, according to Bloomberg, the national refineries received a significant discount after the sanctions laid on the Russian commodity by G7 and the European Union. Facilitated supply of oil and oil derivatives, resources which gave an impulse to inflationary pressure in 2022, should have helped India’s economy to weather the storm. This section examines the key digits for the country’s economy and explains what kind of economic turbulence haunted India throughout the year and why is it of interest to estimate the performance of the stocks selected with the use of Piotroski F-score in these conditions.

The stumbling stone for the Indian and for the global economies in 2022 was inflation rate getting out of control. The main reasons for inflationary pressure stemmed from the trinity of negative factors: protracted period of mild monetary policy called upon to boost the economies during COVID-19 pandemics, supply chain disruptions attributed to harsh quarantine policies in China, and, last but not least, Russia-Ukrainian war causing tensions in the commodities’ market. The mentioned difficulties ended in a surge in consumer price index (CPI), as it increased from 4.35% in September 2021 to its peak of 7.79% in April 2022.
Fig. 4 India’s CPI, 2022, %
Source: Trading Economics: India CPI // Trading Economics, 2023. – URL: https://tradingeconomics.com/india/consumer-price-index-cpi (extracted on 07.02.2023).

The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) took urgent actions and changed the monetary policy mode from dovish to hawkish. The rate hikes started with 40 basis points (bp, 1bp = 0.01%) in April, raising the rates to 4.4% from two-year lows of 4%. The following three rate hikes were all equal to 50bp and took place in June, August, and September, pushing the rate to 5.9%. At the same time, Central bank started clearing its balance sheet, ultimately withdrawing 5 533.49 billion INR from 32 293.65 billion INR in January to the lowest point of 26 760.16 billion INR in October. At the same time, I cannot avoid mentioning that M2 money supply that includes money stored in the personal deposits was steadily increasing throughout the year, climbing up from 51 209 billion INR in January to 55 710.46 billion INR in December. This adverse trend looks quite awkward, since RBI clearly wanted it to shrink, while the authority was struggling with inflation. This growth might have attributed to the Indian stock indexes finally pushing higher in the end of 2022 after the disastrous first half of the year.

Such an aggressive monetary policy bore fruit, as inflation decreased to 5.72% in December, after making a lower spike of 7.41% in June. Both November (5.88%) and December values were in the RBI’s target range of 2-6%, but the RBI Governor Shaktikanta Das claimed that the crusade against inflation was not over and announced a 35bp rate hike in December, ultimately pushing the total interest rate to 6.25% (56.25% increase in one year from 2020 lows).

In spite of the RBI’s efforts, national currency, Indian rupee, depreciated to the US dollar. The currency’s losses slightly exceeded 11% as it fell from 74.46 INR per USD to 82.71 INR per USD. Although India’s economy was healthier than in 2008, the reasons behind the currency depreciation were similar to those during the Global financial crisis: the flight of foreign funds from the Indian market, the slowdown in capital inflows, which decreased the supply of dollars, the higher global crude oil prices, which widened the current account deficit and increased dollar buying by oil companies, and the recovery of the dollar [Singh, 2009, p. 151]. The dynamics of the narrowing capital inflow may be demonstrated through FDI. From the historical perspective, monthly net FDI inflows remained slightly above average, until the drastic decline in August from 5385 million USD in July to only 305 million USD, followed by a strong outflow of 2428 million USD in November that was only the sixth time, when FDI net inflow was negative, since the beginning of the XXI century.

But why did the World Bank give so much credit to the Indian economy in 2022 in spite of the harsh monetary policy? The answer lies in the magnificent gross domestic product (GDP) growth rate in the second quarter. In the last quarter of 2021 released in the beginning of the next year and in the first quarter of 2022, the GDP growth rate equaled to -0.1% and 0%, respectively, almost meeting the rule of thumb definition of recession implying two successive negative quarters in a row [Rogoff, Robinson, Bayoumi, 2002]. Reuters writes that in March, India finally completely opened its economy after the long COVID-19 lockdown that lasted for two years. Moreover, in the first two quarters of 2022, India showed a record government expenditure of 8 756.25 billion INR compared to only 3 358.44 billion INR in the quarter three.

Although the effect from the lockdown cease was quite short-lived (India’s annual GDP growth rate in the third quarter accounted to 6.3% compared to 13.5% in the previous quarter), this performance was still rather solid in comparison to the state of global economy in 2022: the World Bank considers that while one per cent decline in the US growth results in 0.4% decline in the Indian one, it is approximately 0.6% decline for other emerging markets. After all, US economy itself showed two successive quarters of negative GDP growth rate in the beginning of the year, and the yield curve for the US ten-year treasury notes and the US two-year treasury notes eventually became inverted in late March signalizing about the soon economic hardships and, probably, a more severe recession [EPW Research Foundation, 2008]. Having said that, India’s analogical yield curve did not become inverted in 2022, close as it was, when the two-year notes yield rallied roughly 44% from March to November. Therefore, India’s GDP was relatively strong in 2022, but, eventually, recession may be looming over horizon in 2023 already.

Drawing a line under analysis of the fundamental drivers of the Indian economy in 2022, I can conclude that the macroeconomic environment was turbulent, yet not critical. In spite of the continuing RBI’s crusade against inflation, GDP managed to show a very strong performance in the second quarter and money supply M2 remained consistent during the year, allowing the country’s stock market to end the year slightly higher after the downfall in the first half of 2022. The perils for the economy lied in the shocking rate hikes from two-year lows and the Central bank clearing its balance. The performance of the portfolio of stocks selected in accordance with F-score is of interest in 2022 and is in line with the research purpose, since Joseph Piotroski applied F-score to high BM stocks. According to Fama and French [1992, p. 428] cited by Piotroski [2000], high BM is characterized as a variable capturing financial distress, and thus the subsequent returns represent a fair compensation for risk. Generally, in the periods of intense rate hikes these are the companies with low financial health that suffer most, since they start to face more hardships with financing or restructuring their debts. Consequently, India’s 2022 monetary situation puts these stocks under pressure, allowing to assess the screener amidst severe conditions.

sub_elements.title: 2.3. Technical Picture Review
text: In 2022, BSE increased by 4.44% from 58 310.09 to 60 840.74, making a comeback in the second half of the year. From January to June (including), BSE lost 8.99% of value, but regained 14.75% in the next six months. Volatile as it may be, this dynamic is, generally, in line with the macroeconomic analysis presented above: money supply M2 was consistent all along the period, and a positive GDP growth report in the quarter two gave the investors an incentive to buy. However, the market’s perspective for the two following years is vague, as RBI’s monetary policy remains hawkish and recession fears threat to take shape.

The chart hints on the potential double top pattern to be in and the downfall to start in 2023 with the bearish divergence to have formed between the November 2021 and December 2022 peaks and the relative strengths indicator (RSI) values for the two points (while the market produced a higher high in 2022, RSI showed a lower low). Further evidence comes from the moving average convergence divergence (MACD) with the signal line giving a bearish cross in late 2022, therefore also pointing to the lower lows.

Overall positive performance of the broad portfolio of stocks puts to test any portfolio comprised of high BM companies that are likely to experience financial hardships connected with debt financing. Hence, assessing F-score as a screener with Indian stocks in 2022 is of research interest.

At the same time, India VIX hardly reached extreme values in 2022. In 32 weeks, the index closed below 20, and in 16 weeks – below 20 and 30. The monthly proportion is 7:5 in favor of the low-VIX months. The index never closed a week above 30 in 2022. In December 2021, 30 days prior to portfolio formation, the average VIX was equal to approximately 17.18. This number is below 20, thus, according to Woodley, Jones, and Reburn [2011], the F-score is supposed to show robustness, and these are only economic turbulences explained that may prevent the strategy from doing so.

sub_elements.title: 3.1. Sample Description
text: All data necessary to perform the research was acquired from Screener.In, a resource which focuses on small-cap companies listed in India. Overall, the website keeps track on approximately 4 000 companies of all types. There are only 250 companies (6.25%), each capitalization of which exceeds two billion USD (16 497.56 Rs. Cr., 1 Rs. Cr. = 121 230 USD), the traditional low boundary for medium-cap stocks.

Since F-score was designed to estimate value companies, I set the filter to screen stocks with high book-to-market ratio. I define high BM stocks as stocks with BM-ratio above one on January 1st, 2022. Overall, the high BM portfolio included 1 069 companies. Then, the filter is set to search for stocks with F-scores at extreme values back in January 1st, 2022. In his research, Piotroski [2000] defined low and high F-score firms as firms with F-score of 0-1 and of 8-9, respectively, in the end of the year, prior to portfolio formation. Having fulfilled this condition, I received a low F-score portfolio of 17 companies and a high F-score portfolio of 157 companies.

For my research, I have acquired return over the last year for all companies (expressed in per cent) and their respective F-score values. I also downloaded capitalizations of high F-score firms in January, 2022 (expressed in Rs. Cr.) and their average trading volumes (expressed in Cr.). I will use the capitalization data to learn whether its size may influence the stock returns within the target group. Average volume was also studied by Piotroski [2000] and used as an estimate for investors’ interest, I will run regression analysis to understand whether the volume may shift the returns’ distribution.

Since the function of data exporting was unavailable, the data had to be processed manually. Such a meticulous approach certainly increases the probability of mistakes when transmitting the digits from the original screener to Excel; therefore, the test for outliers was performed. For this purpose, I applied interquartile range method. As always, the test comprised the following steps. First, quartile one and quartile three values are obtained. Second, interquartile range is calculated by subtracting the value of quartile one from quartile three. The difference between the value of quartile one and 1.5 interquartile range and the sum of quartile three and 1.5 interquartile range produce lower and upper fences, respectively. After highlighting the numbers below or equal to the lower fence and above or equal to the upper fence, I double-checked all the extreme values. As a result, three mistakes were detected and corrected (incorrect values were substituted with correct).

sub_elements.title: 3.2. Preliminary Hypothesis
text: In the chapter Choice of Country, the annual return of the main Indian stock index BSE in 2022 was provided. In spite of all the economic hardships haunting the country throughout the period, BSE managed to close with 4.4% increase. These are depressed, high BM companies that usually suffer most during the rate hike period, but with the strong second half of the year in the Indian stock market, I expect the annual return of the high BM portfolio to be in the positive territory, but not to beat the index (thus, the MAR should be negative). The previous successes of the high F-score companies should not allow them to overcome return of the entire portfolio significantly, and I suppose MAR to be negative or equal to zero. Finally, the most distressed, low F-score firms should produce strongly negative returns, by far below than those of the broad portfolio and high F-score companies. Obviously, MAR is to be strongly negative as well. Consequently, I expect that the best trading strategy based on the use of F-score in India, 2022 was shorting the low F-score companies. Finally, as for the application of F-scores of 2-7, I suppose that the macroeconomic turbulences will distort the performance of the screener, making its impact on returns insignificant.

sub_elements.title: 3.3. Limitations of the Research
text: Small sample size of the low F-score companies. As is stated above, the sample of the low F-score companies with BM > 1 only has 17 stocks. This quantity is definitely much less than the number of high F-score stocks with BM > 1 (157) and the broad portfolio of high BM stocks (1 069). Piotroski [2000] studied a low F-score portfolio of only 396 stocks, while his broad portfolio consisted of 14 043 companies. This share of low F-score companies is also rather small (1 448 observations were classified as high F-score companies). The two ways to increase the sample size were to study all small companies (irrespective of their BM ratios) or to redefine low F-score stock as a stock with F-score ratio between zero and two, instead of zero to one. Eventually, both ideas were rejected. BM ratio parameter is important, since F-score is not capable of significantly shifting the distribution of returns across low BM companies. The stocks with F-score value of two were left aside as well because when the sample size is increased to 72 companies, the proportion of stocks with F-score value of two strongly displaces the proportion of traditional low F-score firms, thus making the following comparison of portfolios’ performances misleading.

Few medium-cap companies. Since Screener.In specializes solely on small-cap companies, almost no medium-cap stocks were attracted to this research. Out of 250 medium-caps available at the website, only 20 were ultimately used after applying the high BM filter. Piotroski [2000] proved that the greatest returns are concentrated within small-cap companies, yet his F-score is still effective for separating winners from losers within the range of high BM medium-cap companies: these were only big-caps that were almost insensitive to the screener (The improvement in returns is isolated in the bottom two-thirds of market capitalization). The fact that medium-cap companies are not presented in this research is also disappointing because their inclusion could have increased the small sample of low F-score firms.

No information about the analyst coverage. Piotroski [2000] intended to demonstrate the possibility of shifting the distribution of returns to the right by means of pure fundamental strategy that is based on valuing firms based on their financial statements. In an attempt to isolate the studied stocks from all the “white noise”, he proved that high BM firms earning above-market returns display low levels of analyst coverage, and the ability to earn strong returns is negatively correlated to the amount of analyst coverage provided. Unfortunately, the used resource does not provide any information about the analyst coverage. This absence does not undermine the perspective of the research, since high BM stocks are unattractive to the vast majority of investors per se [Piotroski, 2000], yet it would have been interesting to put to trial the criteria of low analyst coverage applied to high BM stocks in the studied period, when such stocks experience additional macroeconomic pressure.

No data for other years of macroeconomic turbulence. Considering the title of this paper, data for 2022 should be enough. Moreover, aggressive monetary tightening in India between 2004 and 2008, or between 2010 and 2012 should have had the same negative effect on the performance of the companies experiencing financial distress as it had in 2022. Having said that, in order to assess F-score amidst macroeconomic turbulence, processing the data over one year is not enough, however coherent the findings may be with the initial hypothesis; otherwise, extrapolating the conclusions from 2022 on the past years or making forecasts based on the current results would be improper and irresponsible. Therefore, more results from the preceding hawkish periods are necessary to add weight to the conclusions.

No information about delisted companies. Unfortunately, the website does not provide any information about companies delisted in 2022. Of course, these firms should be relatively few and far between, yet they might have increased the number of observations, especially for the small sample of the most vulnerable, low F-score companies.

sub_elements.title: 3.4. Methodology of the Research
text: To begin with, I resort to descriptive statistics in order to obtain the general picture of the broad portfolio and the portfolios comprised of low and high F-score companies. The data for the mean, median, standard error, standard deviation, range, skewness and kurtosis is provided. I also calculate the values of the 10th, 25th, 75th, and 90th percentiles, so as to understand how the right and the left tail of the distribution look like.

In order to check whether the screening criteria has impact on the annual return or not, I run regression analysis. Then, since evaluation of extreme values used for screening is key for this research, I compare the means of high and low F-score companies, high F-score companies and the broad portfolio, and low F-score companies and the broad portfolio. For this purpose, I run both classic parametric two-sample t-test for unequal variances and non-parametric bootstrapping.

t-test is regarded to be the best parametric test for examining means of small samples [Bland and Altman, 2009], capable of detecting differences in the means of samples consisting of at least three observations. Therefore, t-test is appropriate for the low F-score portfolio.

As for the bootstrapping approach, I do not use it merely to replicate Joseph Piotroski’s empirical research. The author expressed concerns surrounding the use of parametric test statistics in a long-run return setting (Piotroski [2000] studied the data over a 20-years period from 1976 to 1996). Thus, bootstrapping was used for resampling the broad portfolio and doublechecking the results of the t-tests. In my case, bootstrapping is applied to the low F-score portfolio due to the few observations there. I apply the procedure in the following way. First, I randomly generate 1 000 resampled portfolios of 17 stock returns from the low F-score sample (obviously, repetitions are present). Second, I calculate the means of all these portfolios, together with medians, 10th percentiles, 25th percentiles, 75th percentiles, and 90th percentiles. Finally, the comparison takes place. I calculate the 95th percentile in the obtained distribution of 1000 means of resampled portfolios. If the mean of high F-score portfolio is greater than the 95th percentile, then the mean of high F-score portfolio exceeds the mean of low F-score portfolio in the confidence interval of 95%. Similarly, I also apply confidence intervals of 96%, 97%, 98%, and 99%. The procedure for median and percentiles is the same. When I compare low F-score portfolio with the broad one, instead of the high F-score portfolio, I perform bootstrapping analogically, but now this is the mean, median, and percentiles of the broad portfolio instead of those of the high F-score one that are compared with 1 000 resampled portfolios of low F-score companies.

Finally, I use the regression analysis in order to check if the average annual trading volume and the company’s market capitalization may help to shift the distribution of returns within high F-score portfolio to the right.

sub_elements.title: 3.5. Description of the Research
text: As is seen from the figure with descriptive statistics of the broad portfolio performance below, I underestimated the effect on the high BM stocks produced by the economic shocks in 2022. Broad portfolio failed to close the year in a positive territory with a mean return of -8.6254% and MAR of -13.0254%. The entire left tail of the returns’ distribution of 1 069 companies is strongly negative: 10th and 25th percentiles and median equaled to -49.352%, -34.86%, and -14.51%, respectively. The right tail of 75th and 90th percentiles showed 6.93% and 38.214%, respectively. Consequently, in order to bear fruit, the returns of the high F-score companies should shift the left-tail of the distribution to the positive territory, the possibility which seems very vague at this point.
Fig. 14 Descriptive Statistics of the Broad Portfolio of High BM Stocks

Before processing the returns of the portfolios comprised of low and high F-score companies, I assess the overall influence of the screener on the broad portfolio. The regression analysis presented below (F-score is an independent variable, stock return is a dependent one) demonstrate that the relation between stock returns of high BM companies and their respective F-score values in 2022 is insignificant. Therefore, the extreme monetary policy did bring distortions in the valuation of high BM stocks during this period.
Fig. 15 Regression Analysis of the Influence of F-score on the Broad Portfolio Returns

As for the regression assumptions, I only run Breusch-Pagan test to ensure the homoscedasticity of the data. Multicollinearity and autocorrelation cannot occur because there is only one independent variable and because only one period is examined, respectively. Multivariate normality assumption is not essential in the “sufficiently large samples”, i.e., samples with approximately 100 observations [Lumley et al., 2002, p. 166]. The results of Breusch-Pagan test are presented below and did not detect presence of heteroscedasticity.
Fig. 16 Breusch-Pagan Test for Stock Returns (F-score)

However, the two possible trading strategies based solely on the high BM ratio and F-score are accumulating long positions in the high F-score companies and short positions in the low F-score companies. Therefore, the following results of the stock returns with extreme F-score values are paramount for this research.

The results of the performance of the high F-score portfolio turned out to be very much in line with the preliminary hypothesis. The mean return and MAR accounted to -7.68153% and -12.0815%, respectively. The difference with the results of the broad portfolio is seemingly insignificant. The left tail of the return distribution within this portfolio turned out to be slightly higher than the one within the broad portfolio with 10th and 25th percentiles and median being equal to -47.82%, -30.04%, and -10.99%, respectively. The right tail, on the contrary, did not exceed the values of its counterpart within the broad portfolio, showing 75th percentile of 13.6% and 90th percentile of 33.188%.
Fig. 17 Descriptive Statistics of the High F-score Portfolio

t-tests underpins the conclusion about the means of the high F-score portfolio and the broad portfolio. t-statistics approximately equaled to 0.332, by far falling behind from the t-critical of 1.651. Therefore, the null hypothesis cannot be rejected: there is no significant difference between the mean returns of the two portfolios.
Fig. 18 t-test for High F-score Portfolio and Broad Portfolio

The results produced by the low F-score portfolio also look very promising: these stocks were strongly down in 2022. Mean return and MAR equaled to -26.3441% and -30.7441%, respectively. Both the left and the right tails of the return distribution lie in the negative territory, with only the 90th percentile staying above zero per cent. 10th, 25th, 75th, and 90th percentiles and the median took values of -51.382%, -41.4%, -23.13%, 9.208%, and -32.33%, respectively. The obtained results should be significantly lower than those of the broad portfolio and the high F-score portfolio.
Fig. 19 Descriptive Statistics of the Low F-score Portfolio

Both the t-test and the bootstrapping approach corroborate the conclusions about the returns’ differences of the low F-score portfolio and the broad portfolio and the low F-score portfolio and the high F-score portfolio. The absolute value of the t-statistics for low F-score portfolio and the broad portfolio is 2.681 that is substantially greater than the critical one-tailed t-value of 1.74; hence, null hypothesis about the equality of the means of the two samples is rejected: mean return of the low F-score portfolio is significantly below the mean return of the broad portfolio of the high BM stocks. Bootstrapping approach confirms the result of the t-test. It indicates that the mean of the broad portfolio is greater than the mean of the low F-score portfolio within the 99% confidence interval.
Fig. 20 t-test for Low F-score Portfolio and Broad Portfolio
Fig. 21 Bootstrapping Approach for Low F-score Portfolio and Broad Portfolio

Apparently, the comparison of the high F-score and the low F-score portfolios leads to the similar results. t-test detected the t-value of 2.681 that also overcame the critical one-tailed t of 1.721. Thus, t-test indicates that null hypothesis of no difference is to be rejected in favor of the alternative hypothesis that the mean return of the low F-score portfolio is lower than the mean return of the high F-score portfolio. Bootstrapping approach agrees with this result, stating that the mean return of the high F-score portfolio exceeds the mean return of the low F-score portfolio within the 99% confidence interval.
Fig. 22 t-test for High F-score Portfolio and Low F-score Portfolio
Fig. 23 Bootstrapping Approach for High F-score Portfolio and Low F-score Portfolio

Finally, I assess whether the returns of the high F-score portfolio can be improved when stocks accompanied by substantial “white noise” are removed. Regression analysis of the influence of the market capitalization on the returns of the high F-score portfolio showed that within the studied small-cap companies, the size of capitalization does not have impact on returns, since F-value is 0.94. The same idea can be inferred about the effect of the annual average trading volume. F-value of 0.57 is large enough to break any link between this parameter and the portfolio returns. Once again, the only regression assumption I check in these cases is the absence of heteroscedasticity due to the reasons expressed in the discussion of the first regression studying the dependency of stock returns on the F-score. All data proved to be homoscedastic.
Fig. 24 Regression Analysis of the Influence of Market Capitalization on the High F-score Portfolio Returns
Fig. 25 Breusch-Pagan Test for Stock Returns of High F-score Firms (Market Cap.)
Fig. 26 Regression Analysis of the Influence of Average Trading Volume on the High F-score Portfolio Returns
Fig. 27 Breusch-Pagan Test for Stock Returns of High F-score Firms (1Yr. Average Vol.)

sub_elements.title: 3.6. Results of the Research
text: To draw a line under this chapter, I reiterate that the obtained findings are quite satisfactory and mostly in line with the preliminary hypothesis. The main surprise arose from the mean return of the broad portfolio of high BM stocks: although I expected it to be slightly positive due to the overall decent performance of the Indian stock market in 2022, the hawkish macroeconomic conditions, apparently, took their toll on the distressed firms, eventually leading to the mean value of -8.625%. High F-score companies only slightly surpassed the broad portfolio, finishing the year with the mean return of -7.681%. t-test showed that the difference between two means was statistically insignificant.

The low F-score portfolio, comprised of the companies with the worst past performance, produced a mean return of -26.344% in 2022. Both the parametric t-tests and the non-parametric bootstrapping proved that this mean is less than those of the broad portfolio and the high F-score portfolio. As expected, these companies were more sensitive to the rate hikes than their counterparts from the high BM portfolio; hence, the research proved that building up short positions in them was a wise choice in 2022.

Unfortunately, the regression analysis did not detect any correlation between the market capitalization and the returns of the high F-score firms and the average trading volume and the returns. Perhaps, the influence of the market capitalization could have been more significant if medium-cap and large-cap stocks were included in the studied sample. As for the impact of the trading volume, these might have been some other indicators that show that the stocks are not within investors’ scope and that shift the returns’ distribution. For example, such indicators can be number of flowing analysts, number of shares outstanding, or the stocks’ betas. These contemplations could provide ground for further research.

table_of_content.title: Conclusion
text: The results of the research turned out to be somewhat controversial. On the one hand, the strategy based on the F-score indeed worked: an investor who would open a long position in the high F-score firms and a short position in the low F-score firms on January the 1st 2022 would end the year with a 18.663% return which exceeds the BSE return by 14.223% and the return of the studied portfolio of the high BM stocks by 27.288%. On the other hand, the return of the portfolio of the high F-score firms was negative (-7.681%) and did not significantly differ from the return of the high BM stocks portfolio; therefore, the buy and hold strategy in these allegedly strong companies was inefficient. Furthermore, since the regression analysis did not detect a significant relation between the F-score values and the subsequent stock returns, the F-score should not have been used as an auxiliary indicator coupled with some other signal when the investment decision was taken about the Indian stocks in 2022. I also assessed the two findings by Piotroski [2000] about average trading volume and market capitalization influencing the stock returns of the high F-score firms. Unfortunately, the relations between these criteria and the returns turned out to be insignificant.

The economic situation in the country during the year was turbulent due to an intense monetary tightening started by the RBI. At the same time, lifting COVID-19 restrictions and purchasing Russian commodities at a discount allowed India to show consecutive strength in quarterly GDP data that the stock market positively reacted to: BSE ignored the deterioration in financial conditions and ended the year higher with low volatility in play. While the existing evidence on the F-score advocated for the signal’s success, the essential characteristics of the high BM firms cited in the first chapter were signalizing about the potential problems triggered by the macroeconomics. Therefore, I suppose that the following studies should add macroeconomic conditions (rate hike forecasts, M2 dynamics, national index of financial conditions etc.) in the model to improve the efficiency of the F-score in the short-run.

I address “the following studies” because I cannot extrapolate the findings of this paper on any similar period either in emerging or developed markets because of the limitations discussed in the third chapter. However, the recommendations for value investors relying on the F-score could indeed be produced. Piotroski [2000] applied his indicator to the portfolio of the high BM firms, and Hyde [2013] proved the BM ratio size criteria to be important. High BM firms are often underrated by the market due to their previous failures, but whenever the investors’ pessimism about such stocks becomes excessive, the shares demonstrate solid results. What Piotroski did was identifying those firms, whose financials were improving in the preceding periods. In the long-run, when the signal is coupled with the low VIX levels, satisfactory past financial results promise strong financial results in the current period, thus, strong subsequent stock returns. What this paper proved is that there exists a possibility that these criteria (high F-score and low VIX) are not sufficient to guarantee a further improvement in a company’s performance. Therefore, broadly speaking, what value investors should do is assess whether the macroeconomic conditions favor the continuation of an uptrend.

In particular, India’s financial authorities’ monetary policy in 2022 could bring distortion to three components of the F-score: liquidity, leverage, and equity offer. Although the former two might have been improving (i.e., current ratio and debt-to-assets ratio were increasing and decreasing, respectively) at the date of portfolio formation, company’s liabilities were still large enough to be hampered by the increase in the interest rate, pushing the business to, for example, offer equity to finance its debt, an event which in turn often leads to the decline in stock price, therefore, the signal failure. Another illustration of what might have happened to those investors, who recklessly relied on the F-score in 2022 in India, may lie in the ROA component. Assuming both ROA and change in ROA were positive on January 1st, even the strong GDP data does not guarantee the trend to hold. For instance, India’s government purchase shrank in the third quarter, and, consequently, the companies whose revenue came from the government could suffer, thus, once again depreciating the signal’s predictor power. Analogical hardships may have arisen in any of the nine components of the F-score, and the fact that the mean return of the Indian high F-score firms in 2022 was negative and did not differ significantly from the broad portfolio of the high BM stocks shows that such distortions arose in the majority of cases.

Then, investors should be skeptical about the Piotroski’s “white noise” assumption that could not have aged well in the times of improved information dissemination. The ability of the average trading volume and the number of analysts following to improve the returns of the high F-score firms was not confirmed by the following researches. This paper did not assess the influence of the analyst coverage, but the average trading volume proved to be insignificant. This finding per se cannot be extrapolated on the US markets, since the variations in trading volume among US firms in the period from 1975 to 1996 may have exceeded those of the Indian firms in 2022. However, returning to Piotroski’s assumption about “white noise”, I suppose that with the advent of Internet and social media, analytical information has generally become more available, thus the number of companies that are completely bereft of public attention distorting the information in financial statements is too small to be studied. Consequently, investors should not consider these parameters now, unless additional research proves that they are still actual.

To reiterate, whenever the economic forecasts indicate the near-term turbulence, investors cannot rely on the F-score without subjecting all of its components to the stress-tests. After finding extreme, high F-score and low F-score firms with the help of Piotroski’s screener, all nine terms of the sum should be revaluated from the macroeconomic point of view: whether or not the potential new economic conditions support the further growth or decline. The eternal problem here lies in these “economic forecasts” per se because, so far, producing accurate predictions of the stressful years was no mean feat for the economic science; therefore, the best choice for a canny investor is to always look into the F-score trusting it as a screener, but not as a final justification for adding stocks to one’s portfolio.
